title: Using oTree: A Practical Guide
text:
Welcome
This book serves as an A to Z resource for beginners learning to use oTree to conduct online behavioral experiments and surveys involving human subjects. No prior programming experience is assumed. It is designed to accompany ECON 227B Experimental Economics II lectures and course materials. The detailed syllabus and other course materials are available on the Canvas page for this course.
In this book, you will be introduced to coding simple experiments in the form of “applications” using oTree and deploying them on the Heroku server using oTree Hub. We will begin by installing oTree and Visual Studio Code on your personal laptops and familiarizing ourselves with the VS Code environment. Topics covered include creating an oTree application, executing common oTree tasks, testing code on a local server, running a session with human subjects, coding best practices, and using AI effectively to build your code. This book will also provide a curated list of resources by oTree creators and other users that you can draw on for your individual projects. The point is not to reinvent the wheel, but to stand on the shoulders of giants!

title: Using oTree: A Practical Guide
text:
Prerequisites
Familiarity with Python is helpful but not required — we will build everything from scratch together. If you have never written a line of code before, do not worry. By the end of this course you will be comfortable navigating a coding environment and building your own experiments.

title: Using oTree: A Practical Guide
text:
A Note on This Book
This book will grow alongside the course. Links to reference code and additional materials will be added as we progress through each session. If you are browsing ahead and find session pages that appear empty or incomplete, that is expected — content will be added as we reach those sessions in class.

title: Let’s Get Started!
text:
This section walks you through setting up your development environment before using oTree. Before we meet on April 1st for our first lab session, we request you to download and install the relevant software requirements.
Please ensure you have the following installed on your personal device:

Python — A general-purpose programming language.
oTree — An open-source Python-based framework used to build online interactive experiments.
Visual Studio Code — The Integrated Development Environment (IDE) we will be using for oTree.

Step-by-step instructions for each are provided in the sections below.

title: Setting Up Visual Studio Code
text:
macOS
Visual Studio Code (VS Code) is a free, lightweight code editor we will use throughout this course to write and run oTree code. Follow the instructions below for your operating system.
crumbs: 2  Setting Up Visual Studio Code

title: Setting Up Visual Studio Code
text:
Installation

Go to https://code.visualstudio.com/.
Click Download for Mac.
Once downloaded, open the .zip file — this extracts Visual Studio Code.app.
Drag Visual Studio Code.app into your Applications folder.
Open VS Code from your Applications folder or Spotlight Search (Cmd + Space, type “VS Code”).







Tip



To keep VS Code easily accessible, right-click its icon in the Dock and select Options → Keep in Dock.
crumbs: 2  Setting Up Visual Studio Code

title: Setting Up Visual Studio Code
text:
Windows

Installation

Go to https://code.visualstudio.com/.
Click Download for Windows.
Run the downloaded .exe installer.
Follow the setup wizard and click Install, then Finish.
Launch VS Code from the Start Menu or Desktop shortcut.
crumbs: 2  Setting Up Visual Studio Code

title: Setting Up Visual Studio Code
text:
Installing Extensions
Extensions add functionality to VS Code. For this course, you will need the following:

How to Install an Extension

Open VS Code.
Click the Extensions icon in the left sidebar (or press Ctrl+Shift+X on Windows / Cmd+Shift+X on Mac).
Type the extension name in the search bar.
Click Install.



Required Extensions








Extension
Publisher
Purpose




Python
Microsoft
Core Python language support


Pylance
Microsoft
Improved Python code suggestions and error detection


Python Debugger
Microsoft
Step-through debugging for your oTree code





Recommended Extensions








Extension
Publisher
Purpose




Django
Baptiste Darthenay
Syntax highlighting for oTree HTML templates, which are Django-based


Python Environments
Microsoft
Manage Python virtual environments for your oTree projects


vscode-icons
VSCode Icons Team
Adds file and folder icons for easier project navigation









Note



If you already have VS Code installed, open the Extensions panel and check which of these you already have. You only need to install the missing ones.
crumbs: 2  Setting Up Visual Studio Code

title: Installing Python and oTree
text:
Step 1: Check if Python is Already Installed
oTree is built on Python, so we need to install Python first before installing oTree. Follow the steps below for your operating system.
Before downloading anything, check if Python is already on your system. Open your Terminal (macOS) or Command Prompt (Windows) and run:
macOS — run in Terminal:
Windows — run in Command Prompt:
If you see something like Python 3.10.x or higher, you already have Python and can skip to Step 2.

title: Installing Python and oTree
text:
python3 --version

python --version







Note



If your terminal prompt starts with (base), you have Anaconda or Miniconda installed. This is perfectly fine — Python is already included and you can skip directly to Step 2.

title: Installing Python and oTree
text:
Step 2: Install Python (Only if Needed)

macOS

Go to https://www.python.org/downloads/.
Click Download Python — it will suggest the latest version.
Open the downloaded .pkg file and follow the installer.
Once installed, verify by running:

python3 --version
You should see something like Python 3.12.x. If you do, Python is successfully installed.


Windows

Go to https://www.python.org/downloads/.
Click Download Python — it will suggest the latest version.
Run the downloaded .exe installer.
Important: On the first screen, check the box that says Add Python to PATH before clicking Install Now.







Warning



If you skip checking Add Python to PATH, Python will not be recognized in the terminal and the next steps will not work. If you already installed Python without this box checked, uninstall it and reinstall with the box checked.



Once installed, verify by running:

python --version
You should see something like Python 3.12.x. If you do, Python is successfully installed.

title: Installing Python and oTree
text:
Step 3: Install oTree
Once Python is confirmed, install oTree using pip, Python’s built-in package installer.
macOS — run in Terminal:
pip3 install otree --upgrade
Windows — run in Command Prompt:
pip install otree --upgrade
This may take a minute or two as pip downloads and installs oTree and its dependencies. The --upgrade flag ensures you get the latest version even if oTree was previously installed on your system.

title: Installing Python and oTree
text:
Step 4: Verify oTree Installation
Once the installation is complete, verify it by running the following in your Terminal or Command Prompt:
oTree --version
You should see 6.0.13 or similar. If you do, you are all set!

title: Installing Python and oTree
text:
Troubleshooting







Problem
Fix




python3: command not found (macOS)
Install Python from python.org


python: command not found (Windows)
Reinstall Python and check Add Python to PATH


pip3: command not found (macOS)
Run python3 -m ensurepip in Terminal


oTree: command not found
Run pip3 install otree --upgrade again and restart Terminal









Tip



If you are stuck before the first session, you can use ZotGPT — UCI’s free AI platform — to troubleshoot. Select the latest Claude model available and describe your error message. It is particularly good at solving software installation issues.
You can also bring your laptop to the first lab session on April 1st and we will troubleshoot together.

title: Session 1
text:
In this session, we build the foundation for everything that follows in this course. We start by getting comfortable with VS Code and the terminal, then move into creating and understanding the structure of an oTree project, and finish with a hands-on exercise where you will download, configure, and run a real oTree app.
By the end of this session, you will be able to:

Navigate your file system using the terminal and create a new oTree project and app from scratch.
Read and make sense of what is inside an oTree project folder, and understand the key components of the settings.py file.
Download an app shared by someone else, add it to your project, update settings.py to include it, and run a working demo in your browser.

This session has three parts:








Part
Title
What you will do




Getting Started
Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps
Learn the core terminal commands for working with oTree


Inside an oTree Project
Inside an oTree Project: Folder Structure and settings.py
Understand what oTree creates and how to configure it


Hands-On
Hands-On: Adding and Running Your First App
Download, configure, and demo a shared oTree app

title: Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps
text:
Opening VS Code and navigating to the right folder
This part covers the core terminal commands you will use every time you work in oTree. By the end of this part, you will be able to create a new oTree project and app from scratch, run the development server, and navigate your project folder in VS Code.
Before creating an oTree project, decide where on your computer you want to store your work. A good habit is to keep all your oTree projects inside a dedicated folder — for example, a folder called oTree_lab inside your Documents folder.
To open that folder in VS Code:
You should now see the folder listed in the Explorer panel on the left side of the screen.
crumbs: 4  Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps

title: Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps
text:
Open VS Code
Click File → Open Folder
Navigate to your chosen folder and click Open
crumbs: 4  Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps

title: Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps
section: Opening the terminal in VS Code
text:
Opening the terminal in VS Code
Almost everything in oTree is done through the terminal. In VS Code, open a terminal by clicking Terminal → New Terminal from the top menu bar. A terminal panel will appear at the bottom of the screen.






NoteWhat is the terminal?



The terminal is a text-based interface where you type commands directly to your computer. You will use it constantly in oTree — to navigate between folders, create projects and apps, and run the server.
crumbs: 4  Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps

title: Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps
text:
Navigating folders: cd
The terminal always has a “current directory” — the folder it is currently pointed at. Before running any oTree commands, make sure the terminal is pointed at the right place.
The command cd (short for “change directory”) is how you move around.
Navigate into a folder:
cd Documents/oTree_lab
Go back to your home directory:
cd ~






WarningAvoid spaces in folder names



If any folder in your path has a space in its name, wrap the entire path in quotes:
cd "Documents/my projects/oTree_lab"
The safest habit is to never use spaces in folder or file names at all — use underscores instead (e.g., oTree_lab).
crumbs: 4  Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps

title: Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps
text:
Finding your working directory: pwd
Before running any oTree command, you need to know exactly where the terminal is pointed. The command pwd (print working directory) tells you the full path of your current location.
pwd
You will see output like this:
/Users/nidhipandey/Documents/GitHub/all-otree-code
This is the single most useful command for diagnosing navigation errors. If a cd command fails with “no such file or directory,” run pwd first to confirm where you actually are before trying again.






WarningThe Explorer panel and the terminal are not always in sync



The VS Code Explorer panel shows the folder you have open in VS Code. The terminal shows the folder it is currently pointed at. These are two separate things and they can easily get out of sync — especially if you open the terminal from a different location or run cd ~ and navigate elsewhere.
Always check pwd if the terminal and the Explorer panel seem to be showing different things.
crumbs: 4  Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps

title: Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps
section: Navigating to a folder using the VS Code terminal
text:
Navigating to a folder using the VS Code terminal
The safest and most reliable way to make sure the terminal is pointed at the right folder is to let VS Code do it for you.
The recommended workflow:

Open VS Code
Click File → Open Folder and select the folder you want to work in — for example, all-otree-code
Click Terminal → New Terminal

VS Code will automatically open the terminal pointed at the folder you have open in the Explorer panel. You will see the folder name in the terminal prompt confirming this.
From there, if you want to navigate into a subfolder — for example oTree_lab — just run:
cd oTree_lab
This works every time because the terminal is already inside the parent folder. You do not need to type out the full path.






ImportantAlways open a new terminal from inside VS Code



If you open a terminal from outside VS Code — for example from macOS Spotlight or the Applications folder — it will start pointed at your home directory ~, not at your project folder. You will then have to navigate manually using the full path, which is easy to get wrong.
The habit to build: open VS Code first, open the right folder, then open the terminal from inside VS Code.








TipWhen cd says “no such file or directory”



This error does not always mean the folder does not exist. It usually means the terminal cannot see it from its current location. Run pwd to check where you are, then build the correct path from there. For example, if pwd returns /Users/yourname/Documents/GitHub/all-otree-code, then a folder called oTree_lab inside it is reachable with:
cd oTree_lab
But a folder somewhere else entirely on your computer will not be reachable with just its name. You must navigate to its parent folder first, or use the full path:
cd /Users/yourname/Documents/GitHub/all-otree-code/oTree_lab
crumbs: 4  Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps

title: Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps
section: Starting a new oTree project: otree startproject
text:
Starting a new oTree project: otree startproject






ImportantProject vs. App — know the difference



An oTree project is the top-level container for your experiment. It holds the settings.py file and all of your apps. You will typically only create one project per course or research project. This is a rare task; most of the times you would be creating new apps within an existing project.
An oTree app is a single task or module within your experiment (e.g., a survey, a game, a payment screen). You will create new apps regularly.
Make sure you know which one you need before running a command.


Navigate to your desired folder in the terminal, then run:
otree startproject PROJECT_NAME
Replace PROJECT_NAME with a name of your choice — no spaces or special characters. For example:
otree startproject oTree_lab
oTree will ask whether you want sample games included:
Include sample games? (y or n):
Type y and press Enter. This is useful for your first project as it gives you working examples to explore.
Once the project is created, navigate into it:
cd oTree_lab

What just got created?
Click on the image below to see what the oTree project folder that you created looks like.








Figure 1: VS Code after running otree startproject oTree_lab. The Explorer panel (left) shows the newly created project folder containing the settings.py file and all sample app folders. The terminal at the bottom shows the full sequence of commands run to create and navigate into the project. Note: the code for starting a new project should be written in the terminal of the folder you want the project to live in.





Open the Explorer panel in VS Code and look at your new project folder. Here are the key things to know:







Item
What it is




settings.py
The control center for the entire project. You will edit this regularly.


_rooms/
Where room configuration files go. Do not touch unless instructed.


_static/
Where shared static files (images, CSS, JS) go.


_templates/
Where shared HTML templates go.


Sample app folders
The sample games oTree installed for you.
crumbs: 4  Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps

title: Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps
section: Starting a new app: otree startapp
text:
Starting a new app: otree startapp
Make sure your terminal is pointed at your project folder, then run:
otree startapp APP_NAME
For example:
otree startapp my_survey
This creates a new folder called my_survey inside your project. Inside it you will find:

__init__.py — the Python file where all the logic for your app lives
No HTML files yet — you will create those yourself as you build out your pages







TipName your apps carefully



App names become part of the URL that participants see during your experiment. Use short, lowercase names with underscores and no special characters. Avoid names that reveal anything about your experimental design (e.g., do not call an app treatment_group).
crumbs: 4  Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps

title: Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps
text:
Running the server: otree devserver
To preview your project in the browser, run:
otree devserver
You will see output like this in the terminal:
Open your browser to http://localhost:8000/
To quit the server, press Control+C.
Open your browser and go to http://localhost:8000/demo to see the oTree Demo page. All the sample games will be listed there. Click on any one to try it out.






WarningAlways quit the server when you are done



When you are finished, go back to the terminal and press Ctrl + C to stop the server. Do not just close the browser window — always quit the server first.
crumbs: 4  Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps

title: Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps
text:
Resetting the database: otree resetdb
Whenever you make changes to model variables in your app or update settings.py, you need to reset the database before running the server again:
otree resetdb
oTree will ask you to confirm:
This will delete and recreate your database.
Proceed? (y or n): y






ImportantDo not reset if you have real session data



Resetting the database permanently deletes all session data. If you have collected data from real participants, download it before running otree resetdb. You cannot recover it afterward.
crumbs: 4  Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps

title: Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps
text:
Zipping your project: otree zip
The otree zip command packages your entire oTree project into a single .otreezip file. This is useful for sharing your project with collaborators, submitting it for a class, or uploading it to a server for deployment.
Make sure your terminal is pointed at your project folder, then run:
otree zip
oTree will create a file called PROJECT_NAME.otreezip in the same folder. For example, if your project is called my_experiment, the output will be:
Created my_experiment.otreezip
You can share this file directly with anyone who has oTree installed. To unzip and run it, they simply run:
otree unzip my_experiment.otreezip






TipWhen to zip



Zip your project whenever you want to share it or submit it. It is also a good habit to zip and save a copy of your project before making major changes — think of it as a quick manual backup.
crumbs: 4  Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps

title: Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps
text:
Summary of terminal commands



Command
What it does




pwd
Print the full path of your current directory


cd FOLDER_NAME
Navigate into a folder


cd ~
Return to your home directory


otree startproject NAME
Create a new oTree project


otree startapp NAME
Create a new app inside a project


otree devserver
Start the local development server


otree resetdb
Reset the database


otree zip
Package the project into a .otreezip file


otree unzip FILE.otreezip
Unpack a zipped project


Ctrl + C
Stop the server
crumbs: 4  Getting Started: VS Code, Projects, and Apps

title: Inside an oTree Project: Folder Structure and settings.py
text:
The oTree project folder
Now that you have created your first oTree project, it is time to understand what is actually inside it. Every decision about how your experiment runs — which apps are included, how many participants can demo it, what the payment structure looks like — flows through one file: settings.py. This part explains the project folder layout and walks through the key components of settings.py so you know what to edit, what to leave alone, and why.
When you run otree startproject and include sample games, oTree generates a ready-to-use project folder. Click on the image below to see what that looks like in VS Code.
Figure 1: The oTree project folder oTree_lab open in VS Code. The Explorer panel shows the full contents of the project: the special system folders (_rooms, _static, _templates), the pre-installed sample app folders (such as bargaining, prisoner, survey, and others), and the settings.py file at the bottom of the list. The terminal confirms that the project was created and the working directory is now inside oTree_lab.
Here is a breakdown of every item you will see in a new oTree project:
crumbs: 5  Inside an oTree Project: Folder Structure and `settings.py`

title: Inside an oTree Project: Folder Structure and settings.py
text:
Item
What it is
Do you edit it?




settings.py
The control center for the entire project
Yes — regularly


_rooms/
Stores room configuration files for live sessions
Only when instructed


_static/
Shared static files (images, CSS, JS) used across apps
As needed


_templates/
Shared HTML templates used across apps
As needed


App folders (e.g., survey/)
Each folder is one oTree app
Yes — this is where you build


requirements.txt
Lists Python packages the project depends on
Rarely


Procfile
Server configuration file
Do not touch


LICENSE
License file
Do not touch









WarningSpecial folders vs. app folders



The three folders that begin with an underscore — _rooms/, _static/, and _templates/ — are system folders, not apps. Do not confuse them with your app folders. Never delete or rename them unless instructed by your lab administrator.
crumbs: 5  Inside an oTree Project: Folder Structure and `settings.py`

title: Inside an oTree Project: Folder Structure and settings.py
text:
The settings.py file
The settings.py file is the control center for your entire oTree project. Every app you want to run, every payment parameter, and every session configuration lives here. You will edit this file regularly throughout the course.
Click on the image below to see what a typical settings.py file looks like.








Figure 2: The settings.py file for an oTree project open in VS Code. The file contains several distinct sections: SESSION_CONFIGS (edited regularly), SESSION_CONFIG_DEFAULTS (edit with intention as needed), PARTICIPANT_FIELDS and SESSION_FIELDS (edited with intention as needed), and system-level settings such as SECRET_KEY and INSTALLED_APPS (never touch these). Each section serves a different purpose in controlling how your experiment runs.






SESSION_CONFIGS: Your session list
SESSION_CONFIGS is a Python list of dictionaries. Each dictionary defines one session — one experiment you can run or demo from the admin interface. Think of it as the menu of sessions available in your project.
Here is what a SESSION_CONFIGS list looks like:
SESSION_CONFIGS = [
    dict(
        name='guess_two_thirds',
        display_name="Guess 2/3 of the Average",
        app_sequence=['guess_two_thirds', 'payment_info'],
        num_demo_participants=3,
    ),
    dict(
        name='survey',
        app_sequence=['survey', 'payment_info'],
        num_demo_participants=1,
    ),
]
Every session dictionary requires three key-value pairs:







Key
What it does




name
A unique internal identifier for the session. No spaces or special characters.


app_sequence
A Python list of app names in the order participants will complete them.


num_demo_participants
How many participants are created when you run a demo of this session.









TipChoosing num_demo_participants



Set this number based on your experimental design. If an app groups participants in pairs, use a multiple of 2. If it groups them in threes, use a multiple of 3. For individual decision-making tasks, 1 is enough to test the app.


You can also include optional keys in any session dictionary:







Optional key
What it does




display_name
The name shown in the admin interface. If omitted, name is used.


real_world_currency_per_point
Overrides the default exchange rate for this session only.


participation_fee
Overrides the default participation fee for this session only.





SESSION_CONFIG_DEFAULTS: Project-wide defaults
SESSION_CONFIG_DEFAULTS is a single dictionary that sets default values inherited by all sessions unless a session dictionary explicitly overrides them. The two most commonly used defaults are real_world_currency_per_point and participation_fee.
SESSION_CONFIG_DEFAULTS = dict(
    real_world_currency_per_point=1.00,
    participation_fee=10.00,
    doc=""
)
If you want one specific session to use a different exchange rate or participation fee, simply add that key to that session’s dictionary in SESSION_CONFIGS and it will override the default for that session only.






ImportantLab participation fees



If you run sessions through the ESSL lab, you are bound by the lab’s participation fee policy. Make sure the participation_fee in your settings reflects the approved rate before running any real sessions.




PARTICIPANT_FIELDS and SESSION_FIELDS
These two settings are lists that become relevant in more advanced experiments where you need to carry data from one app to the next — for example, storing a participant’s treatment assignment in the first app and reading it back in the payment screen.

Use PARTICIPANT_FIELDS for data that is specific to an individual participant (e.g., their assigned treatment condition).
Use SESSION_FIELDS for data that applies equally to everyone in the session.

For now, you can leave these as empty lists:
PARTICIPANT_FIELDS = []
SESSION_FIELDS = []
You will learn how to use them properly in later sessions when your experiments involve multiple apps.


DEBUG
You do not currently have this in your settings.py file. Add DEBUG = 0 to your file settings.py under SESSION_FIELDS:
PARTICIPANT_FIELDS = []
SESSION_FIELDS = []

DEBUG = 0
What DEBUG does
DEBUG controls whether oTree runs in development mode or production mode.








Value
Mode
What it means




DEBUG = 1
Development mode
Extra tools and error information are turned on


DEBUG = 0
Production mode
Clean participant-facing interface, no extra tools



Why DEBUG = 1 is useful when building your app
When you are writing and testing your code, setting DEBUG = 1 gives you two very useful advantages:

Auto-advance buttons — On every page, oTree adds a small button that lets you skip past that page without manually filling in any form fields — pages are submitted with empty or default values instead. This lets you click through the entire experiment in seconds to check that the page flow works correctly, without having to fill in every input every time.
Detailed error messages — If your Python code crashes, oTree shows you a full error traceback directly in the browser instead of just a blank page. This makes it much faster to find and fix problems in your code.

These two features make the development cycle dramatically faster. Instead of filling in every form field on every page just to check one thing at the end of the experiment, you can click through the whole app in a few seconds.
Why you must switch to DEBUG = 0 before running real sessions
When DEBUG = 1, participants can also see the auto-advance buttons and skip past pages without submitting their answers. This means your data will be incomplete or entirely missing. Always confirm that DEBUG = 0 before inviting any real participants.






WarningDo not run real sessions with DEBUG = 1



If DEBUG = 1 is on during a live session, participants will see a skip button on every page that lets them bypass your forms — if they use it, their answers will not be recorded. Always confirm DEBUG = 0 before starting any real data collection.








TipA useful development habit



While building and testing your app: set DEBUG = 1. Before running any real session: set DEBUG = 0. This single switch is one of the most common sources of data collection errors for new oTree users — do not forget it.




What you should never touch
Some parts of settings.py must not be modified under any circumstances:







Setting
Why you leave it alone




from os import environ
System import required by oTree — removing it breaks the project


SECRET_KEY
Security key for the project — changing it will break everything


INSTALLED_APPS
Registers oTree with Django — do not modify


ADMIN_PASSWORD
Managed via environment variables by your administrator











Figure 3: The end of the settings.py file for an oTree project open in VS Code. Do not change system-level settings such as SECRET_KEY and INSTALLED_APPS. Each section serves a different purpose in controlling how your experiment runs.











WarningWhen in doubt, do not touch it



If a line in settings.py is not covered in this guide, leave it exactly as it is. Only edit the parts you understand. When in doubt, ask before making any changes.
crumbs: 5  Inside an oTree Project: Folder Structure and `settings.py`

title: Inside an oTree Project: Folder Structure and settings.py
text:
Summary








Section
What it controls
How often you edit it




SESSION_CONFIGS
Which sessions and apps are available
Regularly


SESSION_CONFIG_DEFAULTS
Default payment and session parameters
Occasionally


PARTICIPANT_FIELDS
Cross-app participant-level data
When needed


SESSION_FIELDS
Cross-app session-level data
When needed


USE_POINTS
If True, earnings are shown in points and converted using the exchange rate. If False, real currency is used directly
When needed — based on your design


DEBUG
0 = production mode (normal). 1 = debug mode (shows extra info on participant pages)
Leave at 0 unless developing app or troubleshooting


System settings
Security, server configuration
Never
crumbs: 5  Inside an oTree Project: Folder Structure and `settings.py`

title: Your First App: Adding, Registering, and Understanding __init__.py
text:
Step 1: Download and add the demographic_survey app
In this session you will add a pre-built app to your oTree project, register it in settings.py, demo it in the browser, and then open the code to understand how an oTree app is structured from the inside. By the end of this part you will be able to read and navigate any __init__.py file and understand what each component does.
The example code for this course is stored in a shared Google Drive folder. Navigate to the link below and download the file called demographic_survey.zip:
Example Code — Google Drive
Once downloaded:
After moving it, open VS Code and check the Explorer panel. You should now see demographic_survey listed alongside the other app folders inside your project.
crumbs: 6  Your First App: Adding, Registering, and Understanding `__init__.py`

title: Your First App: Adding, Registering, and Understanding __init__.py
text:
Locate demographic_survey.zip in your Downloads folder
Double-click the file to unzip it — this will produce a folder called demographic_survey
Move the demographic_survey folder into your oTree project folder — for example, into oTree_lab








WarningThe folder must be inside the project folder



The demographic_survey folder must sit directly inside your oTree project folder — at the same level as settings.py and the other app folders. If it is placed anywhere else, oTree will not be able to find it.
crumbs: 6  Your First App: Adding, Registering, and Understanding `__init__.py`

title: Your First App: Adding, Registering, and Understanding __init__.py
section: Step 2: Register the app in SESSION_CONFIGS
text:
Step 2: Register the app in SESSION_CONFIGS
Adding the folder to your project is not enough on its own. oTree only runs apps that are listed in SESSION_CONFIGS inside settings.py. Open settings.py and add the following dictionary to your SESSION_CONFIGS list:
SESSION_CONFIGS = [
    # ... your existing session dictionaries ...

    dict(
        name='survey_leveled_up',
        display_name="End of experiment questionnaires",
        app_sequence=['demographic_survey'],
        num_demo_participants=1,
        doc="Standard end of experiment questionnaires."
    ),
]
Notice the five key-value pairs in this dictionary:








Key
Value
What it does




name
'survey_leveled_up'
Internal identifier used by the server


display_name
"End of experiment questionnaires"
The name shown in the admin interface


app_sequence
['demographic_survey']
The list of apps in this session — just one here


num_demo_participants
1
One participant is enough to demo a survey


doc
"Standard end of experiment questionnaires."
A short note describing the session — optional but good practice



Save settings.py after making the change.






ImportantReset the database after editing settings.py



Any time you edit settings.py — including adding a new session — you must reset the database before running the server. In the terminal, make sure you are inside your project folder, then run:
otree resetdb
Confirm with y when prompted.
crumbs: 6  Your First App: Adding, Registering, and Understanding `__init__.py`

title: Your First App: Adding, Registering, and Understanding __init__.py
text:
Step 3: Demo the app
With the database reset, start the development server:
otree devserver
Open your browser and go to http://localhost:8000/demo. You should now see End of experiment questionnaires listed on the Demo page. Click on it to launch a demo session and work through the app as a participant would. When you are done, press Ctrl + C in the terminal to stop the server.
crumbs: 6  Your First App: Adding, Registering, and Understanding `__init__.py`

title: Your First App: Adding, Registering, and Understanding __init__.py
section: Step 4: Open __init__.py and understand the structure
text:
Step 4: Open __init__.py and understand the structure
Every oTree app has exactly one __init__.py file. This single Python file contains everything the app needs to run: the constants, the data models, the page logic, and the page sequence. Open demographic_survey/__init__.py in VS Code. Here is the full file:
from otree.api import *

doc = """
Standard, end-of-session survey.
Includes Cognitive Reflection Task and demographic survey.
"""

class C(BaseConstants):
    NAME_IN_URL = 'survey_experiment'
    PLAYERS_PER_GROUP = None
    NUM_ROUNDS = 1


class Subsession(BaseSubsession):
    pass


class Group(BaseGroup):
    pass


class Player(BasePlayer):
    age = models.IntegerField(
        label='What is your age?',
        min=18,
        max=99,
    )
    gender = models.StringField(
        choices=[['Male', 'Male'], ['Female', 'Female'],
                 ['Non-Binary', 'Non-Binary'],
                 ['NoState', 'Prefer not to state']],
        label='What is your gender?',
        widget=widgets.RadioSelect,
    )
    race = models.StringField(
        choices=[
            ['Black', 'African descent, Black, African-American'],
            ['Asian', 'Asian descent, Asian-American'],
            ['Indigenous', 'Indigenous, First Nations, Native American'],
            ['MiddleEastern', 'Middle Eastern or North African'],
            ['Hawaiian', 'Native Hawaiian or Pacific Islander'],
            ['White', 'White'],
            ['Other', 'Other'],
            ['Declined', 'Decline to state']
        ],
        label='What is your race?',
        widget=widgets.RadioSelect,
    )
    ethnicity = models.BooleanField(
        choices=[[True, 'Yes'], [False, 'No']],
        label='Are you of Latinx/o/a or Hispanic origin?',
        widget=widgets.RadioSelect,
    )
    school = models.StringField(
        choices=[
            ['Social', 'School of Social Sciences'],
            ['Ecology', 'School of Social Ecology'],
            ['Law', 'School of Law'],
            ['Humanities', 'School of Humanities'],
            ['Education', 'School of Education'],
            ['Arts', 'Claire Trevor School of the Arts'],
            ['Business', 'The Paul Merage School of Business'],
            ['Biological', 'School of Biological Sciences'],
            ['Engineering', 'The Henry Samueli School of Engineering'],
            ['Computers', 'Donald Bren School of Information and Computer Sciences'],
            ['Interdisciplinary', 'Interdisciplinary Studies'],
            ['Physical', 'School of Physical Sciences'],
            ['Medicine', 'School of Medicine'],
            ['Nursing', 'Sue and Bill Gross School of Nursing'],
            ['Pharmaceutical', 'School of Pharmacy and Pharmaceutical Sciences'],
            ['Health', 'Program in Public Health']
        ],
        label='Choose the most appropriate option for your school below.',
        widget=widgets.RadioSelect,
    )
    field_of_study = models.StringField(
        label='What is your major or field of study?',
    )
    major_STEM = models.BooleanField(
        choices=[[True, 'Yes'], [False, 'No']],
        label='Are you majoring in a STEM field?',
        widget=widgets.RadioSelect,
    )
    math_courses = models.IntegerField(
        label='How many Mathematics/Statistics courses have you taken since High School?',
        min=0,
        max=99,
    )
    english_is_first_language = models.BooleanField(
        choices=[[True, 'Yes'], [False, 'No']],
        label='Is English your first language?',
        widget=widgets.RadioSelect,
    )
    first_language = models.StringField(
        label='Please state your first language.',
        blank=True,
    )

    # ~~~ Cognitive Reflection Task

    crt_q1 = models.StringField(
        label="If John can drink one barrel of water in 6 days, and Mary can drink one barrel of water in 12 days, how many days would it take them to drink one barrel of water together?"
    )
    crt_q2 = models.StringField(
        label="A man buys a pig for \$60, sells it for \$70, buys it back for \$80, and sells it finally for \$90. How much money has he made in dollars?"
    )
    crt_q3 = models.StringField(
        label="Jerry received both the 15th highest and the 15th lowest mark in the class. How many students are in the class?"
    )


# ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ PAGES

class Questionnaire_CRT(Page):
    form_model = 'player'
    form_fields = ['crt_q1', 'crt_q2', 'crt_q3']

    @staticmethod
    def is_displayed(player):
        return player.round_number == C.NUM_ROUNDS


class Demographics(Page):
    form_model = 'player'
    form_fields = ['age', 'gender', 'race', 'ethnicity', 'school',
                   'field_of_study', 'major_STEM', 'math_courses',
                   'english_is_first_language', 'first_language']


page_sequence = [Questionnaire_CRT, Demographics]
Now let us go through every part of this file.


The import statement
from otree.api import *
This line is the same in every single oTree app. It imports all of oTree’s built-in classes, field types, widgets, and functions. You will never change this line.



The doc string
doc = """
Standard, end-of-session survey.
Includes Cognitive Reflection Task and demographic survey.
"""
The doc variable is an optional description of the app. It appears in the admin interface when you click on the app sequence for a session. It has no effect on how the app runs — it is purely informational. Write something useful here so that anyone looking at the admin interface understands what this app does without having to open the code.



Class C: Constants
class C(BaseConstants):
    NAME_IN_URL = 'survey_experiment'
    PLAYERS_PER_GROUP = None
    NUM_ROUNDS = 1
The C class holds all the fixed parameters of your app — values that are set before the session starts and do not change while it runs.








Constant
Value
What it means




NAME_IN_URL
'survey_experiment'
The string that appears in the participant’s browser URL during the experiment


PLAYERS_PER_GROUP
None
Participants are not grouped — this is an individual task


NUM_ROUNDS
1
Participants go through the page sequence once









WarningParticipants can see NAME_IN_URL



Whatever string you assign to NAME_IN_URL will be visible in the browser address bar during the experiment. Notice that this app uses 'survey_experiment' rather than 'demographic_survey' — this is deliberate. The name does not reveal what kind of survey is being administered. Do not use names that hint at the purpose or design of your study.








TipPython naming conventions



Constant variables like NAME_IN_URL are written in UPPERCASE_WITH_UNDERSCORES. Non-constant variables and function names use lowercase_with_underscores. Class names use PascalCase (e.g., BasePlayer, WaitPage). VS Code’s colour-coding works better when you follow these conventions.





Data model classes: Subsession, Group, and Player
Every __init__.py must define three data model classes. These represent the three levels of oTree’s data structure and correspond directly to tables in the database.
class Subsession(BaseSubsession):
    pass

class Group(BaseGroup):
    pass
Both Subsession and Group contain only pass here because this app collects individual participant data only. No group-level or round-level variables are needed.






NoteWhat pass means



In Python, pass is a placeholder that means “this class exists but has nothing extra defined in it.” oTree requires all three model classes to be present in every __init__.py. If you are not adding any fields or methods to Subsession or Group, you must still include the class definition with pass underneath it. Removing either class entirely will crash the app.





The Player class and model fields
The Player class is where all the participant-level data lives. Every field you define here becomes a column in your exported data.

Field types used in this app








Field type
Used for
Example in this app




models.IntegerField()
Whole numbers
age, math_courses


models.StringField()
Text or multiple choice
gender, race, school, field_of_study


models.BooleanField()
True/False answers
ethnicity, major_STEM, english_is_first_language





Field arguments used in this app







Argument
What it does




label
The question or prompt shown to the participant on the page


min / max
Sets the minimum and maximum values for numeric fields


choices
A list of options for multiple choice questions


widget
Controls how the input is displayed — widgets.RadioSelect shows radio buttons


blank
If True, the field is optional and participants can skip it





How choices works
This is one of the most important things to understand about oTree model fields. The choices argument takes a list of two-item lists, where each inner list follows this pattern:
choices = [[recorded_in_database, shown_to_participant], ...]
Look at the gender field:
gender = models.StringField(
    choices=[['Male', 'Male'], ['Female', 'Female'],
             ['Non-Binary', 'Non-Binary'],
             ['NoState', 'Prefer not to state']],
    label='What is your gender?',
    widget=widgets.RadioSelect,
)
The participant sees four options on screen: Male, Female, Non-Binary, and Prefer not to state. But notice that 'NoState' is what gets recorded in the database — not 'Prefer not to state'. The first value in each pair is what oTree saves; the second is what the participant reads.
Now look at the ethnicity field:
ethnicity = models.BooleanField(
    choices=[[True, 'Yes'], [False, 'No']],
    label='Are you of Latinx/o/a or Hispanic origin?',
    widget=widgets.RadioSelect,
)
The participant sees Yes or No. The database records True or False. This is a clean and efficient way to store binary responses — it makes analysis much easier than storing the strings “Yes” and “No” directly.






ImportantThe choices format is always [recorded, displayed]



The first value in each pair is stored in the database. The second value is what the participant sees. Never mix these up. If you do, your data will contain the wrong values and you will not catch the error until analysis.




The blank=True argument
first_language = models.StringField(
    label='Please state your first language.',
    blank=True,
)
By default, all form fields in oTree are required — participants cannot proceed to the next page without filling them in. Setting blank=True makes a field optional. Here first_language is optional because only participants who answered No to english_is_first_language need to complete it.


Section comments
# ~~~ Cognitive Reflection Task
This is just a Python comment — a line beginning with # that oTree ignores entirely. It is there purely for readability, separating the demographic fields from the CRT fields. Use comments freely in your own code to mark where one section ends and another begins.




Page classes
Each page in your app is defined as a class that inherits from Page. This app has two pages.

Questionnaire_CRT
class Questionnaire_CRT(Page):
    form_model = 'player'
    form_fields = ['crt_q1', 'crt_q2', 'crt_q3']

    @staticmethod
    def is_displayed(player):
        return player.round_number == C.NUM_ROUNDS







Attribute or method
What it does




form_model = 'player'
Tells oTree the form fields belong to the Player model


form_fields
Lists the three CRT fields that will appear as inputs on this page


@staticmethod
Required decorator before any Page-level method when working in VS Code or any IDE


is_displayed
A built-in oTree method that controls whether this page is shown to a participant. It returns True or False.



The is_displayed method here returns:
return player.round_number == C.NUM_ROUNDS
Since C.NUM_ROUNDS = 1, this evaluates to player.round_number == 1, which is always True for a single-round app. In a multi-round app, this would restrict the page to only the final round. For now it is effectively always displayed.






Tip@staticmethod is required for Page methods in VS Code



When you define any built-in oTree method inside a Page class — such as is_displayed, vars_for_template, or before_next_page — you must precede it with @staticmethod. This is because these methods are defined at the Page level rather than outside it. If you forget @staticmethod, the method will either not work or will produce an error.




Demographics
class Demographics(Page):
    form_model = 'player'
    form_fields = ['age', 'gender', 'race', 'ethnicity', 'school',
                   'field_of_study', 'major_STEM', 'math_courses',
                   'english_is_first_language', 'first_language']
This page has no special methods — just form_model and form_fields. All ten demographic fields will appear on this page in the order they are listed in form_fields. There is no is_displayed method, so this page is always shown.




The page_sequence list
page_sequence = [Questionnaire_CRT, Demographics]
This list controls the order in which participants see the pages. Participants will complete the CRT first, then the demographics questionnaire. If you swap the order here, they will see the demographics first.






WarningEvery page must be in page_sequence



If you define a Page class but forget to add it to page_sequence, oTree will not show it to participants. Conversely, if you list a page in page_sequence that does not have a corresponding class definition, the app will crash when you try to run it.
crumbs: 6  Your First App: Adding, Registering, and Understanding `__init__.py`

title: Your First App: Adding, Registering, and Understanding __init__.py
text:
Summary: The anatomy of __init__.py








Section
What it contains
Required?




from otree.api import *
The oTree import statement
Always


doc
A short description of the app
Optional but recommended


class C(BaseConstants)
NAME_IN_URL, PLAYERS_PER_GROUP, NUM_ROUNDS
Always


class Subsession
Round-level logic and fields
Always — use pass if empty


class Group
Group-level logic and fields
Always — use pass if empty


class Player
Individual participant fields
Always — add your data fields here


Page classes
One class per page, with form_model, form_fields, and any methods
Always — at least one


page_sequence
The ordered list of page classes
Always
crumbs: 6  Your First App: Adding, Registering, and Understanding `__init__.py`

title: Useful Resources
text:
oTree
This is a curated list of resources to support your learning throughout this course. You do not need to master all of these — bookmark what is useful and return to it as needed.
The official oTree documentation is the most reliable resource for all questions about oTree. It is well-written, actively maintained, and covers fundamental concepts of what you will need.
Always check oTree’s demo apps and common task recipes before you start writing code from scratch — sample code has likely already been written for what you are trying to do. The Featured Projects section on oTree Hub is a great place to start:
Both the old and the new oTree forums are useful sources of solutions to common problems. Note that the old forum contains solutions written in oTree’s older self format, so some details may need to be adapted to the newer no-self format used in this course. See here for more details on how the new format differs from the old one.
Finally, the Public projects section on oTree Hub hosts code from other oTree users that is shared publicly.
Here is the complete list of oTree resources:

title: Useful Resources
text:
Game Theory Sample Games — Classic games from economics and game theory (Prisoner’s Dilemma, etc.). Useful for understanding how standard experiments are structured in oTree. Includes a live demo and source code.
More Demos — A wide range of demo apps from different disciplines. A good starting point for finding code that is close to what you want to build. Includes a live demo and source code.
Snippets — A collection of small recipes for common tasks in oTree. If you need to do something specific, check here first. Includes a live demo and source code(https://github.com/oTree-org/otree-snippets).





Official oTree documentation
Featured Projects on oTree Hub
oTree’s “Where do I put this code?” breakdown
New oTree forum
Old oTree forum
Public projects







TipSearching for oTree help



When you run into a problem and are not sure where to start, try Googling your task or error message together with the word “otree” — for example, “otree timer page” or “otree wait for all players”. This will surface relevant results from the documentation, forums, and other oTree users.
If you find a result on Stack Overflow, read the full discussion before implementing any solution — do not just copy the first answer you see. The comments often reveal important nuance, and your use-case may require a slightly different approach.

title: Useful Resources
text:
Python
Python is the backbone of your oTree app — it handles the logic, stores data, and communicates between the server and the participant’s browser. A quick tip: use print() to see variable values and outputs in the terminal while you are debugging your code.
Web-based editors (for testing small bits of code)
The following editors are useful when you want to quickly test a small piece of Python code without running it through oTree. This list is adapted from resources originally compiled by Faith Bague-Sampson.

Python Fiddle
myCompiler: Python
PYnative Online Python Editor
